items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: When energy is 'lost' as heat due to friction, what has really happened?
items.correct: Energy has been transferred to the thermal store of the surroundings — it's not destroyed, just dissipated.
items.misconception: Energy has been used up and no longer exists.
items.random_wrong: Energy has been converted into mass following E=mc^2.
items.why: Everyday language ('used up', 'lost') and bills ('energy used') reinforce the idea energy is consumed, contradicting conservation.

items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.

items.q: A 2 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s². What is the resultant force?
items.why: It's 8 N. “2 N” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does it mean if the resultant force on an object is zero?
items.correct: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.. “The object must be stationary.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Calculate the weight in newtons of a 5 kg mass on Earth (g = 9.8 N/kg).
items.why: The real figure is 49 N. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
